name: Thoth
shortDescription: Egyptian god of wisdom, writing, magic, and the moon, often depicted with an ibis head or as a baboon.
description: Thoth, in Egyptian mythology, is the god of wisdom, writing, hieroglyphs, science, magic, art, judgment, and the moon. Depicted as an ibis-headed man, a baboon, or a man with an ibis head holding a writing palette, Thoth was considered the scribe of the gods and the inventor of writing. He maintained the library of the gods, possessed immense knowledge, and was believed to have authored the Book of Thoth, a collection of magical spells and wisdom. Thoth played a crucial role in the weighing of the heart ceremony in the afterlife, recording the results and ensuring cosmic balance (Ma'at). He mediated disputes between gods, and his wisdom was essential for maintaining order in the universe. His influence permeated various aspects of Egyptian life, from scribal practices to religious rituals and the understanding of the cosmos.
subtitle: Scribe of the Gods, Keeper of Wisdom
role: Maintaining cosmic order, recording events, and guiding souls

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Thoth's origins are ancient, predating much of the established Egyptian pantheon. He was believed to have been self-created, or born from the seed of Horus from the forehead of Set. As the inventor of writing, Thoth is credited with creating the hieroglyphic script, which Egyptians believed held magical power. He features prominently in the myth of Isis and Osiris, where he helps Isis resurrect Osiris by using his magical knowledge. Thoth also assisted Horus in his battles against Set, providing strategic advice and magical support. He is often associated with the moon, representing its cycles and linking him to timekeeping and calendar systems.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Thoth's role extended to virtually every aspect of Egyptian life. Scribes, who held a high position in society, revered him as their patron deity. Temples dedicated to Thoth served as centers of learning and writing. His presence was essential in the afterlife, where he recorded the judgment of the deceased in the Hall of Ma'at. Thoth's ability to maintain cosmic balance and mediate disputes made him a vital figure in the Egyptian worldview. He ensured that Ma'at (truth, justice, and cosmic order) prevailed, both in the mortal realm and in the afterlife. Without Thoth, chaos would reign.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Thoth's symbolism is rich and multifaceted. The ibis, his most common representation, was associated with wisdom and knowledge due to its long beak, which was seen as a symbol of careful and precise thought. The baboon, another of his forms, symbolized intelligence and was often depicted greeting the rising sun, representing enlightenment. The lunar associations of Thoth connect him to cycles, time, and the measurement of the year. His role as a scribe symbolizes the power of language and the importance of recording knowledge for future generations. The writing palette and stylus he often holds represent his mastery of writing and his role as the divine record keeper.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Thoth's influence extended beyond ancient Egypt. The Greeks associated him with Hermes, leading to the syncretic figure of Hermes Trismegistus, a legendary sage to whom various esoteric texts were attributed. The Hermetic tradition, based on these texts, influenced alchemy, astrology, and other esoteric practices in the Western world. In modern times, Thoth remains a figure of interest in occultism, neopaganism, and New Age spirituality. His association with wisdom, knowledge, and magic continues to resonate, and he is often invoked as a symbol of intellectual pursuit and spiritual enlightenment. He also appears in popular culture, in books, movies, and video games.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Egyptian deities, including detailed information on Thoth's attributes and roles.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: A classic work providing detailed accounts of Egyptian mythology and religious beliefs, including Thoth's significance.

associations.name: Ibis
associations.significance: Symbolizes wisdom, knowledge, and careful observation.

associations.name: Baboon
associations.significance: Symbolizes intelligence and greeting the dawn of knowledge.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Represents cycles, time, and the measurement of the year.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Hermes
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: God of writing, communication, magic, and messenger of the gods; associated with intellect and wisdom.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Ogma
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: God of eloquence, literature, and language, credited with inventing the Ogham script.

companions.name: Ma'at
companions.description: Goddess of truth, justice, and cosmic order; Thoth ensures her principles are upheld.

companions.name: Seshat
companions.description: Goddess of writing, record-keeping, and measurement; often associated with Thoth in scribal activities.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Writing Materials
worshipPractices.description: Scribes would offer writing implements and papyrus to Thoth in temples dedicated to him.

worshipPractices.name: Hymns and Prayers
worshipPractices.description: Devotees would recite hymns and prayers to Thoth, seeking his wisdom and guidance.

festivals.name: Festival of Thoth
festivals.timing: First day of the Egyptian year
festivals.description: Celebrated Thoth's role as the inventor of writing and the keeper of knowledge, marking the beginning of a new cycle.

cultural.modernLegacy: Thoth's legacy persists in esoteric traditions, neopaganism, and popular culture, symbolizing wisdom, knowledge, and the power of language. He frequently appears in modern fantasy literature and games.
